title: Graph terminology and models

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Graph terminology and models; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A graph models vertices and directed or undirected edges, optionally with weights, labels, self-loops, or parallel edges.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A course prerequisite graph is directed because A requiring B does not imply B requires A. Your goal is to model a real relationship problem as vertices and edges with justified graph properties. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Stacks, queues and deques; Hash tables, sets and maps; Algorithm specification and invariants; pf data selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A graph models vertices and directed or undirected edges, optionally with weights, labels, self-loops, or parallel edges. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Problem semantics decide whether direction, multiplicity, and disconnected vertices matter. The draft therefore rejects: Every relationship graph should be treated as undirected for simpler traversal.

includes: Distinguish directed, undirected, weighted, cyclic, connected and simple graph properties.
Model a real relationship problem as vertices and edges with justified graph properties.

In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
excludes: Problem semantics decide whether direction, multiplicity, and disconnected vertices matter.

outcomes: Model a real relationship problem as vertices and edges with justified graph properties.

examples.title: Graph terminology and models: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Problem semantics decide whether direction, multiplicity, and disconnected vertices matter.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
